rationale: The citing paper uses Tuli et al. (2010) in its hypothesis development on relational overlap, specifically to establish that relational multiplexity can have benefits in dyadic relationships. It then builds a context-specific argument that, in acquisitions, inherited multiplex ties can create integration and partner-management burdens that reduce acquisition performance. Because the citation helps set up and qualify the core theoretical logic for H2, it plays a meaningful conceptual role rather than a passing background mention.
relation_description: The citing paper takes Tuli et al.'s multiplexity insight as a baseline and then qualifies it for acquisition settings, arguing that multiplex ties created via acquirer-target overlap can produce coordination costs that offset or reverse expected benefits. Thus, it narrows the conditions under which multiplexity is performance-enhancing.
